title: Tetanus
oneLiner: A toxin-mediated, life-threatening neuromuscular disease causing painful muscle rigidity and spasms, primarily in unvaccinated or elderly patients.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:** A 65-year-old unvaccinated man presents to the ED with a three-day history of jaw stiffness that has progressed to difficulty opening his mouth (trismus). He reports stepping on a rusty nail in his garden two weeks ago; the wound looked clean and healed, but he did not seek medical care. On examination, he has a fixed, "risus sardonicus" smile, generalized rigidity of abdominal muscles, and mild opisthotonos when lying supine. His neck is stiff, and his vital signs show tachycardia (110 bpm) and mild hypertension (145/90). Any attempt to move him triggers painful, generalized muscle spasms.

**Dissecting the vignette:**
- *Jaw stiffness → trismus:* Masseter muscle spasm – earliest sign in ~75% of tetanus.
- *Difficulty opening mouth:* Classic "lockjaw" – pathognomonic for generalized tetanus.
- *Rusty nail + unvaccinated:* High-risk inoculation; Clostridium tetani spores in soil/environment.
- *Two-week incubation:* Typical 3–21 days; shorter if wound is heavily contaminated.
- *Healed wound:* Tetanus occurs even after wound appears clean – spores germinate in devitalized tissue.
- *Risus sardonicus:* Sustained facial muscle spasm -> raised eyebrows, retracted lips.
- *Opisthotonos:* Back arching due to paraspinal spasm – severe form.
- *Tachycardia/hypertension:* Autonomic instability from disinhibited sympathetic drive.
- *Triggered spasms:* Stretch or sound precipitates tetanic contractions – a key diagnostic clue.

**Atypical presentations:**
1. **Cephalic tetanus** – isolated cranial nerve palsy (e.g., facial nerve) after head wound. May mimic Bell's palsy; later progresses to trismus.
2. **Neonatal tetanus** – newborn (day 3–14) presents with poor feeding, irritability, then generalized rigidity and opisthotonos. Almost always from umbilical cord contamination (unsterile delivery).
3. **Localized tetanus** – persistent muscle spasm at the wound site only (e.g., leg rigidity). Rarely progresses to generalized form; can be mistaken for post-traumatic dystonia.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
Tetanus is an acute, toxin-mediated infection caused by *Clostridium tetani*, an anaerobic, spore-forming Gram-positive bacillus found ubiquitously in soil, dust, and manure. The spores germinate in contaminated wounds (especially deep, devitalized tissue) and produce tetanospasmin, a neurotoxin that blocks inhibitory neurotransmitter release, leading to unchecked muscle excitation and autonomic instability.

**Why it matters clinically:** Tetanus is rare in developed countries due to vaccination (DTP/Tdap), but it remains a killer globally (~200,000 deaths/year, mostly neonates). You will see it in unvaccinated individuals (elderly, immigrants, religious objectors) or those with waning immunity (adults >60 years). The stakes are high: mortality is 10–20% even in modern ICUs. The common pitfall is missing the early signs (trismus, dysphagia, neck stiffness) and attributing them to dental or ENT pathology – delaying life-saving antitoxin and supportive care.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The story:** *C. tetani* spores enter a wound (often trivial). In the presence of low oxygen (necrosis, foreign body, co-infection), spores germinate into vegetative bacteria that produce tetanospasmin. This toxin is a 150-kDa protein that travels via retrograde axonal transport inside motor neurons to the spinal cord and brainstem. It then trans-synaptically enters inhibitory interneurons (e.g., Renshaw cells in the spinal cord) and targets the presynaptic membrane to cleave synaptobrevin (a SNARE protein), preventing release of glycine and GABA (inhibitory neurotransmitters).

**Mechanism → Sign:**
- *Blocked GABA/glycine release → disinhibition of alpha motor neurons → continuous, simultaneous firing of agonist and antagonist muscles → rigidity.* This explains trismus, opisthotonos, and abdominal wall spasm.
- *Loss of reciprocal inhibition → spasms triggered by sensory stimuli (touch, sound, light).* The usual "clasp-knife" response to stretch is replaced by a violent, painful contraction.
- *Autonomic centers in brainstem/spinal cord also lose inhibition → sympathetic overactivity:* tachycardia, hypertension, sweating, arrhythmias, and finally hypotension (dysautonomia). This is the late, lethal phase.
- *Local tetanus:* Toxin remains confined to one motor neuron pool – e.g., only leg muscles.
- *Cephalic tetanus:* Toxin enters cranial nerves (commonly VII) via head wound → isolated facial palsy.

**Connect to pattern recognition:**
- Trismus is masseter spasm from disinhibition of motor nucleus V.
- Risus sardonicus = facial muscles (VII) spasm.
- Opisthotonos = paraspinal/extensor rigidity (more antigravity muscles).
- Spasms triggered by stretch = loss of reciprocal inhibition (the "triggered" nature is a hallmark).
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Stem clue | Notes |
|----------|-----------|-------|
| **Wound contamination (most common)** | "Stepped on a rusty nail / gardening injury / puncture wound" | Spores in soil, manure, dust. Any break in skin. Deeper wounds = more anaerobic. |
| **Injection drug use** | "IVDU with skin popping" | Subcutaneous injections introduce spores into devitalized tissue. Very common in low-resource settings. |
| **Surgery/trauma** | "Post-operative wound infection / compound fracture" | Even sterile surgeries can introduce spores if skin not cleaned (rare). |
| **Burns** | "Burn wound in an unvaccinated patient" | Devitalized tissue, prolonged healing. |
| **Neonatal (umbilical)** | "Newborn day 5-10 – poor feeding, rigidity" | Unsterile cord cutting; unvaccinated mother. |
| **Ottitis media** | "Chronic ear infection with head/neck rigidity" | Rare; spores from ear canal enter mastoid/middle ear → cephalic tetanus. |
| **Dental infections** | "Tooth abscess with trismus" | May mimic odontogenic infection, but trismus is out of proportion. |

**Most common cause overall:** Puncture wounds (especially feet) contaminated with soil/dust in an unvaccinated individual. Always ask: "Have you had a tetanus shot? Any recent cuts, scratches, or animal bites?"
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnosis is clinical:** No lab test confirms tetanus in acute phase. However, you must perform investigations to rule out mimics and assess severity.

**Scoring system:** The **Ablett** or **Phillips** classification for tetanus severity:
- **Grade I (mild):** Mild trismus, mild rigidity, no spasms.
- **Grade II (moderate):** Moderate trismus, rigidity, short spasms (<30s), mild dysphagia.
- **Grade III (severe):** Severe trismus, rigidity, prolonged spasms, autonomic instability (tachycardia, hypertension).
- **Grade IV (very severe):** Grade III + autonomic instability causing hypotension, arrhythmias, or cardiac arrest.

**Investigations:**

| Test | What it shows | Why you order it | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|------------------|----------------|
| **Wound culture (anaerobic)** | *C. tetani* growth | Confirms organism (but takes days; sensitivity 30%) | Positive = definitive, but negative does not rule out. |
| **Serum tetanus antitoxin level** | Antibody titer (IU/mL) | Protects >0.1 IU/mL. Low titer = susceptible | <0.1 IU/mL indicates inadequate immunity. |
| **CBC / CRP / Procalcitonin** | Leukocytosis, normal or elevated inflammatory markers | Rule out sepsis; tetanus is an intoxication, not sepsis. | WBC may be mildly elevated; high CRP suggests co-infection. |
| **Creatine kinase (CK)** | Elevated in severe muscle damage | Monitor rhabdomyolysis risk. | >5000 U/L may indicate renal failure risk. |
| **Head CT / MRI** | Normal | Rule out intracranial pathology (e.g., stroke, abscess) if cephalic tetanus or atypical. | Normal imaging supports tetanus. |
| **Electromyography (EMG)** | Continuous motor unit activity, loss of silent period | Differentiate from myotonia, stiff-person syndrome. | "Continuous activity" typical. |

**Pattern-recognition:**
- **Confirming finding:** Classic clinical picture (trismus + risus sardonicus + triggered spasms) in a patient with a wound and no vaccination history. No lab test needed.
- **Ruling out:** A negative antitoxin level (<0.1 IU/mL) plus typical exam is diagnostic. The absence of a wound does not exclude tetanus (up to 20% have none).
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour):** *Suspicion is the trigger.* Do not wait for confirmatory tests.

1. **Assess airway and respiratory function:** If trismus/severe dysphagia, early intubation (preferably awake fiberoptic). *Why:* Laryngeal spasm can precipitate at any time; cannot wait until respiratory failure. Use midazolam or propofol for sedation.
2. **Administer human tetanus immune globulin (HTIG):** 500–3000 IU IV once (dose varies by severity; 3000 IU for moderate-severe). *Why:* Neutralizes unbound toxin. It cannot affect toxin already internalized, but prevents progression. **Do not give equine antitoxin (risk of anaphylaxis).**
3. **Wound debridement:** Remove all necrotic tissue and foreign bodies. *Why:* Spores require anaerobic environment; debridement eliminates source of toxin production. **Do not wait; it can be done in ED or theatre.**
4. **Start antibiotics:** Metronidazole 500 mg IV every 8 hours for 7–10 days. Penicillin G 2–4 million units IV q4h is alternative, but metronidazole better. *Why:* Kills vegetative bacteria; toxin production stops.
5. **Sedation and muscle relaxants:** Diazepam 10–40 mg IV boluses (or infusion) for muscle spasms. *Why:* GABA agonist partially restores inhibition. Alternative: baclofen intrathecal or dantrolene (if severe spasms not controlled). In refractory cases: vecuronium or rocuronium + mechanical ventilation.

**Ward (24–72 hours):** *The patient is likely intubated and sedated; focus on prevention of complications.*

- **Continue deep sedation:** Propofol or midazolam infusion. *Why:* Spasms are painful and consume oxygen; aim for RASS -4 to -5.
- **Monitor autonomic instability:** Use esmolol (short-acting beta-blocker) for tachycardia/hypertension; or labetalol. *Why:* Alpha/beta blockade reduces myocardial oxygen demand. Avoid long-acting agents (risk of hypotension later).
- **Provide aggressive nutrition via NG/OG tube.** *Why:* Catabolic state due to rigidity. Caloric needs 25–35 kcal/kg/day.
- **Prevent DVT and GI bleeding:** Enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily; pantoprazole 40 mg IV daily.
- **Complete active immunization:** Give tetanus toxoid (Tdap) once patient stable. *Why:* Natural infection does not confer immunity (toxin dose too small).

**Long-term (post-extubation to discharge):**
- **Rehabilitation:** Physical therapy for contractures; speech therapy for dysphagia.
- **Continue antibiotics for 7–10 days total.**
- **Provide vaccination series:** Tdap now, then Td at 1 and 6 months.

**What NOT to do:**
- Do not give intrathecal antitoxin (no benefit, risk of meningitis).
- Do not use corticosteroids – no proven benefit, increase infection risk.
- Do not stimulate the patient – minimal handling, dark quiet room.
- Do not delay intubation waiting for cyanosis – sudden laryngospasm kills.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Tetanus | Stiff-Person Syndrome | Strychnine Poisoning | Dystonic Reaction (Antipsychotics) |
|---------|---------|-----------------------|----------------------|--------------------------------------|
| **Pain** | None initially; spasm is painful | Mild muscle pain; not triggered | Severe generalized pain | None reported |
| **Exam** | Trismus, risus sardonicus, opisthotonos, triggered spasms | Axial rigidity, no trismus, stimulus does NOT trigger spasms | Generalized rigidity, opisthotonos, risus sardonicus, *preserved consciousness* | Torticollis, oculogyric crisis, tongue spasm; trismus rare |
| **ECG** | Sinus tachycardia, hypertension | Normal | Sinus tachycardia | Normal |
| **Labs** | Normal CK (mild elevation); low tetanus antibody | Anti-GAD antibodies positive (60%) | Normal; blood/urine strychnine positive | Normal; recent antipsychotic use |
| **Key differentiator** | History of wound + vaccination status + trismus + triggered spasms | No wound; trismus absent; anti-GAD positive | Rapid onset (minutes); no wound; strychnine in tox screen | Recent antipsychotic; responds to benztropine; no autonomic instability |

**Other mimics:** *Hypocalcemic tetany* (Chvostek/Trousseau signs, low calcium, no wound), *Meningitis* (nuchal rigidity but positive Kernig/Brudzinski, fever, CSF changes), *Rabies* (hydrophobia, aerophobia, progression to paralysis, animal bite).

**Key pattern:** If you have trismus + wound + no vaccination, stop – it's tetanus.
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**Major complications (in order of decease frequency):**

1. **Respiratory failure:** Laryngeal spasm → acute airway obstruction. *Signs:* Stridor, cyanosis, bradycardia. *What to do:* Immediate intubation (cricothyroidotomy if cannot open mouth). *If missed:* Cardiac arrest, death.
2. **Aspiration pneumonia:** Due to dysphagia + sedation. *Signs:* Fever, hypoxia, new CXR infiltrate. *What to do:* Antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam or ceftriaxone + metronidazole). *If missed:* ARDS, sepsis.
3. **Autonomic storm (dysautonomia):** Labile BP (first hypertension, then hypotension), arrhythmias (tachy, brady, VF), hyperthermia. *Signs:* Sweating, vasodilation, BP swings. *What to do:* Esmolol, magnesium, clonidine; call ICU. *If missed:* Cardiac arrest, multisystem failure.
4. **Rhabdomyolysis:** Sustained muscle contraction → myoglobinuria → acute kidney injury. *Signs:* Dark urine, rising CK, hyperkalemia. *What to do:* IV fluids, bicarb (controversial), monitor potassium. *If missed:* Dialysis.
5. **Venous thromboembolism (DVT/PE):** Immobilization, hypercoagulable state. *Signs:* Unilateral leg swelling, dyspnea, hypoxia. *What to do:* Heparin infusion (if bleeding risk low). *If missed:* Fatal PE.
6. **Contractures:** Untreated rigidity leads to fixed joint deformities. *Late presentation:* Already deformed on arrival. *What to do:* Physical therapy early; may require serial casting.

**Late-presentation pattern (the "if untreated" stem):** A patient found at home with severe opisthotonos, locked jaw, no ability to speak, in respiratory distress, with a old surgical wound that was never cleaned. They die from laryngospasm before reaching ICU. The lesson: suspect tetanus early.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

1. **"TRISMUS"** – T: Trismus, R: Rigidity (abdominal, paraspinal), I: Increased tone, S: Spasms triggered, M: Masseter spasm, U: Unvaccinated, S: Sympathetic instability (autonomic). Useful to remember core features.

2. **"RISUS SARDONICUS"** – R: Retrograde axonal transport of toxin, I: Inhibitory neuron block (GABA/glycine), S: Sustained contraction of face, U: Unopposed motor activity, S: Smile with raised brows; SARDONICUS = Stiffness, Autonomic, Rigidity, Dysphagia, Opisthotonos, Neck stiffness, Intercostal, Chvostek (but not Hypocalcemia), Unilateral (cephalic), Spasms. Overly complicated but memorable.

3. **"DARK SILENT WOUND"** – Debridement, Antitoxin, Resp support, Keep quiet, Sedation, Immunization, Lines (central line for BP control), ETT (intubate early), Nutrition, Tetanus toxoid (vaccinate). Management steps.

**Clinical pearls:**
1. **"Rusty nail" is the classic stem, but any soil/dust contamination works.** Always ask about gardening, farming, or animal excrement.
2. **Trismus is the earliest sign – don't call it dental abscess until you have ruled out tetanus.** Antibiotics for "dental infection" will delay antitoxin.
3. **The "spastic smile" (risus sardonicus) is pathognomonic.** It looks like the patient is smiling but there's no joy – more like a grimace.
4. **Stimulus-triggered spasms are key:** tapping the patient's chest or shining a light may cause a full-body spasm. This is not seen in meningitis or stiff-person syndrome.
5. **Autonomic instability carries the highest mortality.** If you see tachycardia and hypertension, manage aggressively with beta-blockade and call ICU early.
6. **Never give equine antitoxin – always use human (HTIG).** Anaphylaxis to horse serum is catastrophic.
7. **Complete vaccination after recovery:** the tiny amount of toxin does not stimulate immune memory. Give Tdap and a full series.

**Common mistakes to avoid:**
- Not asking about vaccination history (elderly often don't know; assume they need booster if >10 years and wound is dirty).
- Delaying intubation for "mild" trismus – wait until you can't open the mouth enough to pass an endotracheal tube.
- Using succinylcholine for intubation (risk of hyperkalemia from upregulation of ACh receptors in prolonged paralysis) – use rocuronium.
- Forgetting to turn off lights and noise – stimulation sets off spasms.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call senior/ICU/crash team:**

- **Airway compromise:** Any stridor, inability to swallow, or severe trismus (mouth opening < 2 cm) → call anaesthetics/ICU for awake fiberoptic intubation.
- **Spasms refractory to diazepam:** >3 generalized spasms per hour despite 20+ mg IV → need neuromuscular blockade (vecuronium/rocuronium) and ICU admission.
- **Autonomic instability:**
  - Sustained tachycardia > 130 bpm, or hypertension > 180/110 despite sedation → call ICU for esmolol/labetalol.
  - Hypotension (systolic < 90) despite fluids → call ICU; suspect autonomic failure – need vasopressors.
- **NEWS2 trigger:** Score ≥ 7 (or sustained ≥ 5) due to respiratory rate, SpO2, or heart rate → senior review.
- **Lactate > 4 mmol/L** despite fluids → consider septic shock (co-infection) or hypoperfusion from sustained spasm.
- **GCS < 12** – if not intubated, protect airway.
- **Cardiac arrest** – call crash team; cause likely laryngospasm or arrhythmia (tetanus does not cause PEA unless severe hypoxia). Use standard ALS, but be aware of difficult airway.

**Specific thresholds:**
- **Systolic BP > 200 or < 90 mmHg** – call for autonomic management.
- **Heart rate > 140 bpm** – risk of myocardial ischemia; call ICU.
- **Oxygen saturations < 90% on room air** – need supplemental O2 and evaluate for aspiration.
- **Creatine kinase > 10,000 U/L** – risk of AKI; call renal team.
- **Anuria for 6 hours** despite fluid challenge – urgent renal evaluation.

**Remember:** The most senior decision-maker in tetanus is the intensivist. You are buying time while HTIG neutralizes toxin – get to ICU early.

rawMarkdown:
# Tetanus

**One-liner:** A toxin-mediated, life-threatening neuromuscular disease causing painful muscle rigidity and spasms, primarily in unvaccinated or elderly patients.

## 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:** A 65-year-old unvaccinated man presents to the ED with a three-day history of jaw stiffness that has progressed to difficulty opening his mouth (trismus). He reports stepping on a rusty nail in his garden two weeks ago; the wound looked clean and healed, but he did not seek medical care. On examination, he has a fixed, "risus sardonicus" smile, generalized rigidity of abdominal muscles, and mild opisthotonos when lying supine. His neck is stiff, and his vital signs show tachycardia (110 bpm) and mild hypertension (145/90). Any attempt to move him triggers painful, generalized muscle spasms.

**Dissecting the vignette:**
- *Jaw stiffness → trismus:* Masseter muscle spasm – earliest sign in ~75% of tetanus.
- *Difficulty opening mouth:* Classic "lockjaw" – pathognomonic for generalized tetanus.
- *Rusty nail + unvaccinated:* High-risk inoculation; Clostridium tetani spores in soil/environment.
- *Two-week incubation:* Typical 3–21 days; shorter if wound is heavily contaminated.
- *Healed wound:* Tetanus occurs even after wound appears clean – spores germinate in devitalized tissue.
- *Risus sardonicus:* Sustained facial muscle spasm -> raised eyebrows, retracted lips.
- *Opisthotonos:* Back arching due to paraspinal spasm – severe form.
- *Tachycardia/hypertension:* Autonomic instability from disinhibited sympathetic drive.
- *Triggered spasms:* Stretch or sound precipitates tetanic contractions – a key diagnostic clue.

**Atypical presentations:**
1. **Cephalic tetanus** – isolated cranial nerve palsy (e.g., facial nerve) after head wound. May mimic Bell's palsy; later progresses to trismus.
2. **Neonatal tetanus** – newborn (day 3–14) presents with poor feeding, irritability, then generalized rigidity and opisthotonos. Almost always from umbilical cord contamination (unsterile delivery).
3. **Localized tetanus** – persistent muscle spasm at the wound site only (e.g., leg rigidity). Rarely progresses to generalized form; can be mistaken for post-traumatic dystonia.

## 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

Tetanus is an acute, toxin-mediated infection caused by *Clostridium tetani*, an anaerobic, spore-forming Gram-positive bacillus found ubiquitously in soil, dust, and manure. The spores germinate in contaminated wounds (especially deep, devitalized tissue) and produce tetanospasmin, a neurotoxin that blocks inhibitory neurotransmitter release, leading to unchecked muscle excitation and autonomic instability.

**Why it matters clinically:** Tetanus is rare in developed countries due to vaccination (DTP/Tdap), but it remains a killer globally (~200,000 deaths/year, mostly neonates). You will see it in unvaccinated individuals (elderly, immigrants, religious objectors) or those with waning immunity (adults >60 years). The stakes are high: mortality is 10–20% even in modern ICUs. The common pitfall is missing the early signs (trismus, dysphagia, neck stiffness) and attributing them to dental or ENT pathology – delaying life-saving antitoxin and supportive care.

## 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**The story:** *C. tetani* spores enter a wound (often trivial). In the presence of low oxygen (necrosis, foreign body, co-infection), spores germinate into vegetative bacteria that produce tetanospasmin. This toxin is a 150-kDa protein that travels via retrograde axonal transport inside motor neurons to the spinal cord and brainstem. It then trans-synaptically enters inhibitory interneurons (e.g., Renshaw cells in the spinal cord) and targets the presynaptic membrane to cleave synaptobrevin (a SNARE protein), preventing release of glycine and GABA (inhibitory neurotransmitters).

**Mechanism → Sign:**
- *Blocked GABA/glycine release → disinhibition of alpha motor neurons → continuous, simultaneous firing of agonist and antagonist muscles → rigidity.* This explains trismus, opisthotonos, and abdominal wall spasm.
- *Loss of reciprocal inhibition → spasms triggered by sensory stimuli (touch, sound, light).* The usual "clasp-knife" response to stretch is replaced by a violent, painful contraction.
- *Autonomic centers in brainstem/spinal cord also lose inhibition → sympathetic overactivity:* tachycardia, hypertension, sweating, arrhythmias, and finally hypotension (dysautonomia). This is the late, lethal phase.
- *Local tetanus:* Toxin remains confined to one motor neuron pool – e.g., only leg muscles.
- *Cephalic tetanus:* Toxin enters cranial nerves (commonly VII) via head wound → isolated facial palsy.

**Connect to pattern recognition:**
- Trismus is masseter spasm from disinhibition of motor nucleus V.
- Risus sardonicus = facial muscles (VII) spasm.
- Opisthotonos = paraspinal/extensor rigidity (more antigravity muscles).
- Spasms triggered by stretch = loss of reciprocal inhibition (the "triggered" nature is a hallmark).

## 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Stem clue | Notes |
|----------|-----------|-------|
| **Wound contamination (most common)** | "Stepped on a rusty nail / gardening injury / puncture wound" | Spores in soil, manure, dust. Any break in skin. Deeper wounds = more anaerobic. |
| **Injection drug use** | "IVDU with skin popping" | Subcutaneous injections introduce spores into devitalized tissue. Very common in low-resource settings. |
| **Surgery/trauma** | "Post-operative wound infection / compound fracture" | Even sterile surgeries can introduce spores if skin not cleaned (rare). |
| **Burns** | "Burn wound in an unvaccinated patient" | Devitalized tissue, prolonged healing. |
| **Neonatal (umbilical)** | "Newborn day 5-10 – poor feeding, rigidity" | Unsterile cord cutting; unvaccinated mother. |
| **Ottitis media** | "Chronic ear infection with head/neck rigidity" | Rare; spores from ear canal enter mastoid/middle ear → cephalic tetanus. |
| **Dental infections** | "Tooth abscess with trismus" | May mimic odontogenic infection, but trismus is out of proportion. |

**Most common cause overall:** Puncture wounds (especially feet) contaminated with soil/dust in an unvaccinated individual. Always ask: "Have you had a tetanus shot? Any recent cuts, scratches, or animal bites?"

## 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnosis is clinical:** No lab test confirms tetanus in acute phase. However, you must perform investigations to rule out mimics and assess severity.

**Scoring system:** The **Ablett** or **Phillips** classification for tetanus severity:
- **Grade I (mild):** Mild trismus, mild rigidity, no spasms.
- **Grade II (moderate):** Moderate trismus, rigidity, short spasms (<30s), mild dysphagia.
- **Grade III (severe):** Severe trismus, rigidity, prolonged spasms, autonomic instability (tachycardia, hypertension).
- **Grade IV (very severe):** Grade III + autonomic instability causing hypotension, arrhythmias, or cardiac arrest.

**Investigations:**

| Test | What it shows | Why you order it | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|------------------|----------------|
| **Wound culture (anaerobic)** | *C. tetani* growth | Confirms organism (but takes days; sensitivity 30%) | Positive = definitive, but negative does not rule out. |
| **Serum tetanus antitoxin level** | Antibody titer (IU/mL) | Protects >0.1 IU/mL. Low titer = susceptible | <0.1 IU/mL indicates inadequate immunity. |
| **CBC / CRP / Procalcitonin** | Leukocytosis, normal or elevated inflammatory markers | Rule out sepsis; tetanus is an intoxication, not sepsis. | WBC may be mildly elevated; high CRP suggests co-infection. |
| **Creatine kinase (CK)** | Elevated in severe muscle damage | Monitor rhabdomyolysis risk. | >5000 U/L may indicate renal failure risk. |
| **Head CT / MRI** | Normal | Rule out intracranial pathology (e.g., stroke, abscess) if cephalic tetanus or atypical. | Normal imaging supports tetanus. |
| **Electromyography (EMG)** | Continuous motor unit activity, loss of silent period | Differentiate from myotonia, stiff-person syndrome. | "Continuous activity" typical. |

**Pattern-recognition:**
- **Confirming finding:** Classic clinical picture (trismus + risus sardonicus + triggered spasms) in a patient with a wound and no vaccination history. No lab test needed.
- **Ruling out:** A negative antitoxin level (<0.1 IU/mL) plus typical exam is diagnostic. The absence of a wound does not exclude tetanus (up to 20% have none).

## 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour):** *Suspicion is the trigger.* Do not wait for confirmatory tests.

1. **Assess airway and respiratory function:** If trismus/severe dysphagia, early intubation (preferably awake fiberoptic). *Why:* Laryngeal spasm can precipitate at any time; cannot wait until respiratory failure. Use midazolam or propofol for sedation.
2. **Administer human tetanus immune globulin (HTIG):** 500–3000 IU IV once (dose varies by severity; 3000 IU for moderate-severe). *Why:* Neutralizes unbound toxin. It cannot affect toxin already internalized, but prevents progression. **Do not give equine antitoxin (risk of anaphylaxis).**
3. **Wound debridement:** Remove all necrotic tissue and foreign bodies. *Why:* Spores require anaerobic environment; debridement eliminates source of toxin production. **Do not wait; it can be done in ED or theatre.**
4. **Start antibiotics:** Metronidazole 500 mg IV every 8 hours for 7–10 days. Penicillin G 2–4 million units IV q4h is alternative, but metronidazole better. *Why:* Kills vegetative bacteria; toxin production stops.
5. **Sedation and muscle relaxants:** Diazepam 10–40 mg IV boluses (or infusion) for muscle spasms. *Why:* GABA agonist partially restores inhibition. Alternative: baclofen intrathecal or dantrolene (if severe spasms not controlled). In refractory cases: vecuronium or rocuronium + mechanical ventilation.

**Ward (24–72 hours):** *The patient is likely intubated and sedated; focus on prevention of complications.*

- **Continue deep sedation:** Propofol or midazolam infusion. *Why:* Spasms are painful and consume oxygen; aim for RASS -4 to -5.
- **Monitor autonomic instability:** Use esmolol (short-acting beta-blocker) for tachycardia/hypertension; or labetalol. *Why:* Alpha/beta blockade reduces myocardial oxygen demand. Avoid long-acting agents (risk of hypotension later).
- **Provide aggressive nutrition via NG/OG tube.** *Why:* Catabolic state due to rigidity. Caloric needs 25–35 kcal/kg/day.
- **Prevent DVT and GI bleeding:** Enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily; pantoprazole 40 mg IV daily.
- **Complete active immunization:** Give tetanus toxoid (Tdap) once patient stable. *Why:* Natural infection does not confer immunity (toxin dose too small).

**Long-term (post-extubation to discharge):**
- **Rehabilitation:** Physical therapy for contractures; speech therapy for dysphagia.
- **Continue antibiotics for 7–10 days total.**
- **Provide vaccination series:** Tdap now, then Td at 1 and 6 months.

**What NOT to do:**
- Do not give intrathecal antitoxin (no benefit, risk of meningitis).
- Do not use corticosteroids – no proven benefit, increase infection risk.
- Do not stimulate the patient – minimal handling, dark quiet room.
- Do not delay intubation waiting for cyanosis – sudden laryngospasm kills.

## 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Tetanus | Stiff-Person Syndrome | Strychnine Poisoning | Dystonic Reaction (Antipsychotics) |
|---------|---------|-----------------------|----------------------|--------------------------------------|
| **Pain** | None initially; spasm is painful | Mild muscle pain; not triggered | Severe generalized pain | None reported |
| **Exam** | Trismus, risus sardonicus, opisthotonos, triggered spasms | Axial rigidity, no trismus, stimulus does NOT trigger spasms | Generalized rigidity, opisthotonos, risus sardonicus, *preserved consciousness* | Torticollis, oculogyric crisis, tongue spasm; trismus rare |
| **ECG** | Sinus tachycardia, hypertension | Normal | Sinus tachycardia | Normal |
| **Labs** | Normal CK (mild elevation); low tetanus antibody | Anti-GAD antibodies positive (60%) | Normal; blood/urine strychnine positive | Normal; recent antipsychotic use |
| **Key differentiator** | History of wound + vaccination status + trismus + triggered spasms | No wound; trismus absent; anti-GAD positive | Rapid onset (minutes); no wound; strychnine in tox screen | Recent antipsychotic; responds to benztropine; no autonomic instability |

**Other mimics:** *Hypocalcemic tetany* (Chvostek/Trousseau signs, low calcium, no wound), *Meningitis* (nuchal rigidity but positive Kernig/Brudzinski, fever, CSF changes), *Rabies* (hydrophobia, aerophobia, progression to paralysis, animal bite).

**Key pattern:** If you have trismus + wound + no vaccination, stop – it's tetanus.

## 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

**Major complications (in order of decease frequency):**

1. **Respiratory failure:** Laryngeal spasm → acute airway obstruction. *Signs:* Stridor, cyanosis, bradycardia. *What to do:* Immediate intubation (cricothyroidotomy if cannot open mouth). *If missed:* Cardiac arrest, death.
2. **Aspiration pneumonia:** Due to dysphagia + sedation. *Signs:* Fever, hypoxia, new CXR infiltrate. *What to do:* Antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam or ceftriaxone + metronidazole). *If missed:* ARDS, sepsis.
3. **Autonomic storm (dysautonomia):** Labile BP (first hypertension, then hypotension), arrhythmias (tachy, brady, VF), hyperthermia. *Signs:* Sweating, vasodilation, BP swings. *What to do:* Esmolol, magnesium, clonidine; call ICU. *If missed:* Cardiac arrest, multisystem failure.
4. **Rhabdomyolysis:** Sustained muscle contraction → myoglobinuria → acute kidney injury. *Signs:* Dark urine, rising CK, hyperkalemia. *What to do:* IV fluids, bicarb (controversial), monitor potassium. *If missed:* Dialysis.
5. **Venous thromboembolism (DVT/PE):** Immobilization, hypercoagulable state. *Signs:* Unilateral leg swelling, dyspnea, hypoxia. *What to do:* Heparin infusion (if bleeding risk low). *If missed:* Fatal PE.
6. **Contractures:** Untreated rigidity leads to fixed joint deformities. *Late presentation:* Already deformed on arrival. *What to do:* Physical therapy early; may require serial casting.

**Late-presentation pattern (the "if untreated" stem):** A patient found at home with severe opisthotonos, locked jaw, no ability to speak, in respiratory distress, with a old surgical wound that was never cleaned. They die from laryngospasm before reaching ICU. The lesson: suspect tetanus early.

## 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

1. **"TRISMUS"** – T: Trismus, R: Rigidity (abdominal, paraspinal), I: Increased tone, S: Spasms triggered, M: Masseter spasm, U: Unvaccinated, S: Sympathetic instability (autonomic). Useful to remember core features.

2. **"RISUS SARDONICUS"** – R: Retrograde axonal transport of toxin, I: Inhibitory neuron block (GABA/glycine), S: Sustained contraction of face, U: Unopposed motor activity, S: Smile with raised brows; SARDONICUS = Stiffness, Autonomic, Rigidity, Dysphagia, Opisthotonos, Neck stiffness, Intercostal, Chvostek (but not Hypocalcemia), Unilateral (cephalic), Spasms. Overly complicated but memorable.

3. **"DARK SILENT WOUND"** – Debridement, Antitoxin, Resp support, Keep quiet, Sedation, Immunization, Lines (central line for BP control), ETT (intubate early), Nutrition, Tetanus toxoid (vaccinate). Management steps.

**Clinical pearls:**
1. **"Rusty nail" is the classic stem, but any soil/dust contamination works.** Always ask about gardening, farming, or animal excrement.
2. **Trismus is the earliest sign – don't call it dental abscess until you have ruled out tetanus.** Antibiotics for "dental infection" will delay antitoxin.
3. **The "spastic smile" (risus sardonicus) is pathognomonic.** It looks like the patient is smiling but there's no joy – more like a grimace.
4. **Stimulus-triggered spasms are key:** tapping the patient's chest or shining a light may cause a full-body spasm. This is not seen in meningitis or stiff-person syndrome.
5. **Autonomic instability carries the highest mortality.** If you see tachycardia and hypertension, manage aggressively with beta-blockade and call ICU early.
6. **Never give equine antitoxin – always use human (HTIG).** Anaphylaxis to horse serum is catastrophic.
7. **Complete vaccination after recovery:** the tiny amount of toxin does not stimulate immune memory. Give Tdap and a full series.

**Common mistakes to avoid:**
- Not asking about vaccination history (elderly often don't know; assume they need booster if >10 years and wound is dirty).
- Delaying intubation for "mild" trismus – wait until you can't open the mouth enough to pass an endotracheal tube.
- Using succinylcholine for intubation (risk of hyperkalemia from upregulation of ACh receptors in prolonged paralysis) – use rocuronium.
- Forgetting to turn off lights and noise – stimulation sets off spasms.

## 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call senior/ICU/crash team:**

- **Airway compromise:** Any stridor, inability to swallow, or severe trismus (mouth opening < 2 cm) → call anaesthetics/ICU for awake fiberoptic intubation.
- **Spasms refractory to diazepam:** >3 generalized spasms per hour despite 20+ mg IV → need neuromuscular blockade (vecuronium/rocuronium) and ICU admission.
- **Autonomic instability:**
  - Sustained tachycardia > 130 bpm, or hypertension > 180/110 despite sedation → call ICU for esmolol/labetalol.
  - Hypotension (systolic < 90) despite fluids → call ICU; suspect autonomic failure – need vasopressors.
- **NEWS2 trigger:** Score ≥ 7 (or sustained ≥ 5) due to respiratory rate, SpO2, or heart rate → senior review.
- **Lactate > 4 mmol/L** despite fluids → consider septic shock (co-infection) or hypoperfusion from sustained spasm.
- **GCS < 12** – if not intubated, protect airway.
- **Cardiac arrest** – call crash team; cause likely laryngospasm or arrhythmia (tetanus does not cause PEA unless severe hypoxia). Use standard ALS, but be aware of difficult airway.

**Specific thresholds:**
- **Systolic BP > 200 or < 90 mmHg** – call for autonomic management.
- **Heart rate > 140 bpm** – risk of myocardial ischemia; call ICU.
- **Oxygen saturations < 90% on room air** – need supplemental O2 and evaluate for aspiration.
- **Creatine kinase > 10,000 U/L** – risk of AKI; call renal team.
- **Anuria for 6 hours** despite fluid challenge – urgent renal evaluation.

**Remember:** The most senior decision-maker in tetanus is the intensivist. You are buying time while HTIG neutralizes toxin – get to ICU early.
